sourceDrivers: The Fragmenting Public; From Sick Care to Self-Care
seedingQuestion: What still holds people together enough to act on health collectively?
title: Shared civic public
condition: People see health as a common responsibility and accept shared solutions.

sourceDrivers: The Fragmenting Public; From Sick Care to Self-Care
seedingQuestion: What still holds people together enough to act on health collectively?
title: Layered publics
condition: National consensus is weak, but local and identity-based communities coordinate internally.

sourceDrivers: The Fragmenting Public; From Sick Care to Self-Care
seedingQuestion: What still holds people together enough to act on health collectively?
title: Personalized publics
condition: People organize around individualized risks, values, and information feeds.

sourceDrivers: The Fragmenting Public; From Sick Care to Self-Care
seedingQuestion: What still holds people together enough to act on health collectively?
title: No common public
condition: Health is treated mainly as a private responsibility and consumer choice.

sourceDrivers: The Trust Recession; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who do people trust to guide health decisions?
title: Institutional recovery
condition: Public institutions regain trust through performance, transparency, and accountability.

sourceDrivers: The Trust Recession; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who do people trust to guide health decisions?
title: Trust goes local
condition: Clinicians, community health workers, and local leaders become the trusted layer.

sourceDrivers: The Trust Recession; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who do people trust to guide health decisions?
title: Trust moves to platforms
condition: People rely on brands, influencers, and personal AI for health guidance.

sourceDrivers: The Trust Recession; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who do people trust to guide health decisions?
title: Identity-bound trust
condition: People accept guidance mainly from sources aligned with their group.

sourceDrivers: The Epistemic Crisis; The Outsourced Mind
seedingQuestion: How do people decide what health information is real?
title: Verified evidence layer
condition: Authentication and provenance restore a broadly shared factual baseline.

sourceDrivers: The Epistemic Crisis; The Outsourced Mind
seedingQuestion: How do people decide what health information is real?
title: Human-AI mediation
condition: Trusted people and supervised AI translate evidence for different audiences.

sourceDrivers: The Epistemic Crisis; The Outsourced Mind
seedingQuestion: How do people decide what health information is real?
title: Competing evidence systems
condition: Communities use different experts, facts, and standards of proof.

sourceDrivers: The Epistemic Crisis; The Outsourced Mind
seedingQuestion: How do people decide what health information is real?
title: Synthetic fog
condition: False and real content become too difficult to distinguish at scale.

sourceDrivers: Public Health Under Political Fire; The Lowered Shield
seedingQuestion: How much permission does public health have to act?
title: Broad permission to act
condition: Public health has durable civic support and clear legal authority.

sourceDrivers: Public Health Under Political Fire; The Lowered Shield
seedingQuestion: How much permission does public health have to act?
title: Issue-by-issue permission
condition: Authority is accepted for some threats but contested for others.

sourceDrivers: Public Health Under Political Fire; The Lowered Shield
seedingQuestion: How much permission does public health have to act?
title: Place-dependent permission
condition: Legitimacy and powers differ sharply across states and communities.

sourceDrivers: Public Health Under Political Fire; The Lowered Shield
seedingQuestion: How much permission does public health have to act?
title: Constant political resistance
condition: Public health remains a routine target and its powers are tightly constrained.

sourceDrivers: The Great Subtraction; Funding Whiplash & the Austerity Cliff
seedingQuestion: What kind of institutional base can public health sustain?
title: Strong public backbone
condition: Core institutions have durable funding, staff, and infrastructure.

sourceDrivers: The Great Subtraction; Funding Whiplash & the Austerity Cliff
seedingQuestion: What kind of institutional base can public health sustain?
title: Lean core, shared capacity
condition: Small institutions rely on networks, pooled funds, and shared services.

sourceDrivers: The Great Subtraction; Funding Whiplash & the Austerity Cliff
seedingQuestion: What kind of institutional base can public health sustain?
title: Strong nodes, weak gaps
condition: Capable organizations coexist with major geographic and functional holes.

sourceDrivers: The Great Subtraction; Funding Whiplash & the Austerity Cliff
seedingQuestion: What kind of institutional base can public health sustain?
title: Hollowed and outsourced
condition: Critical functions move to vendors or disappear as public capacity shrinks.

sourceDrivers: The Workforce Cliff; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who does the work of public health?
title: Rebuilt public workforce
condition: A broader multidisciplinary workforce is recruited and retained.

sourceDrivers: The Workforce Cliff; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who does the work of public health?
title: Small AI-augmented core
condition: Lean teams work through automation, CHWs, and shared specialists.

sourceDrivers: The Workforce Cliff; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who does the work of public health?
title: Distributed partner workforce
condition: Contractors, community organizations, universities, and creators do more of the work.

sourceDrivers: The Workforce Cliff; The Power of One
seedingQuestion: Who does the work of public health?
title: Chronic shortage and memory loss
condition: Vacancies persist and capability resets with each departure.

sourceDrivers: AI as Public Health Infrastructure
seedingQuestion: Who controls and benefits from public-health AI?
title: Public AI utility
condition: Trusted, governed tools are available across the field.

sourceDrivers: AI as Public Health Infrastructure
seedingQuestion: Who controls and benefits from public-health AI?
title: Shared network services
condition: Institutions pool AI capabilities, talent, and standards.

sourceDrivers: AI as Public Health Infrastructure
seedingQuestion: Who controls and benefits from public-health AI?
title: Vendor-owned stack
condition: Agencies rent closed tools and infrastructure from private firms.

sourceDrivers: AI as Public Health Infrastructure
seedingQuestion: Who controls and benefits from public-health AI?
title: Unequal AI arms race
condition: Well-resourced actors accelerate while others fall further behind.

sourceDrivers: Data Modernization & Real-Time Surveillance; The Shadow Healthcare System
seedingQuestion: How visible is population health in real time?
title: Live public health picture
condition: Integrated systems provide a trusted, real-time view of population health.

sourceDrivers: Data Modernization & Real-Time Surveillance; The Shadow Healthcare System
seedingQuestion: How visible is population health in real time?
title: Connected local pictures
condition: Federated systems share enough data to coordinate while remaining decentralized.

sourceDrivers: Data Modernization & Real-Time Surveillance; The Shadow Healthcare System
seedingQuestion: How visible is population health in real time?
title: Private platforms see more
condition: Companies have faster and richer data than public agencies.

sourceDrivers: Data Modernization & Real-Time Surveillance; The Shadow Healthcare System
seedingQuestion: How visible is population health in real time?
title: Growing blind spots
condition: Fragmented systems and off-grid care leave public health partially blind.

sourceDrivers: Biodata Sovereignty
seedingQuestion: Who owns and controls data from people's bodies?
title: Trusted public use
condition: Strong governance allows biodata to support population health.

sourceDrivers: Biodata Sovereignty
seedingQuestion: Who owns and controls data from people's bodies?
title: Individual control
condition: People decide who can use their data and may trade or license it.

sourceDrivers: Biodata Sovereignty
seedingQuestion: Who owns and controls data from people's bodies?
title: Corporate control
condition: Platforms and data brokers hold the most valuable datasets.

sourceDrivers: Biodata Sovereignty
seedingQuestion: Who owns and controls data from people's bodies?
title: Government access
condition: Public agencies or law enforcement gain broad access for public or political purposes.

sourceDrivers: Chronic, Metabolic & Longevity Reckoning; From Sick Care to Self-Care

sourceDrivers: Chronic, Metabolic & Longevity Reckoning; From Sick Care to Self-Care

sourceDrivers: Chronic, Metabolic & Longevity Reckoning; From Sick Care to Self-Care

condition: Wellness products, personal data, supplements, and biotech define prevention.

sourceDrivers: Chronic, Metabolic & Longevity Reckoning; From Sick Care to Self-Care

sourceDrivers: The Power of One; The Fringe Ascendant
